---
title: "Correction-factor derivation: the 'subtract 1' step"
date: 2026-06-13
format: html
---

## Question

In the absolute-threshold correction derivation (supplement
`#sec-supp-corr-lt50`, Step 5), how does

$$\frac{\text{up}-\text{low}}{0.5-\text{low}} - 1$$

become $\dfrac{\text{up}-0.5}{0.5-\text{low}}$? The confusion was reading the
"$-1$" as "$\times-1$" rather than as **subtracting one**.

## Answer

It is a plain subtraction. Write the `1` over the **same denominator** so the two
fractions combine:

$$1 = \frac{0.5-\text{low}}{0.5-\text{low}}.$$

Then

$$\frac{\text{up}-\text{low}}{0.5-\text{low}} - \frac{0.5-\text{low}}{0.5-\text{low}}
= \frac{(\text{up}-\text{low}) - (0.5-\text{low})}{0.5-\text{low}}.$$

Expand the numerator, distributing the minus sign over the second bracket:

$$(\text{up}-\text{low}) - (0.5-\text{low}) = \text{up} - \text{low} - 0.5 + \text{low} = \text{up} - 0.5,$$

because $-\text{low} + \text{low} = 0$. Hence

$$\frac{\text{up}-\text{low}}{0.5-\text{low}} - 1 = \frac{\text{up}-0.5}{0.5-\text{low}}.$$

## Why it matters

The `low` cancels only in the numerator; the denominator keeps $0.5-\text{low}$.
So the asymmetry correction
$\frac{1}{k}\log\frac{\text{up}-0.5}{0.5-\text{low}}$ measures how far `up` sits
**above** 0.5 relative to how far `low` sits **below** it. When the curve is
symmetric about 0.5 (i.e. $\text{up}-0.5 = 0.5-\text{low}$) the ratio is 1, its
log is 0, and the correction vanishes — the absolute $\log_{10}t_{50}$ then
coincides with the midpoint `mid(T)` (see `#sec-supp-corr-vanish`).

## Numerical check

```{r}
up <- 0.92; low <- 0.05
lhs <- (up - low) / (0.5 - low) - 1
rhs <- (up - 0.5) / (0.5 - low)
c(lhs = lhs, rhs = rhs, equal = isTRUE(all.equal(lhs, rhs)))
```
